2017 · Nature · Post-training & RL

Mastering the game of Go without human knowledge

AlphaGo Zero throws out the human expert game records that bootstrapped the original AlphaGo. A single network, given only the raw board and the rules, learns Go entirely by playing itself — and beats the Lee Sedol-defeating AlphaGo 100 games to 0.

David Silver, Julian Schrittwieser, Karen Simonyan, Ioannis Antonoglou, Aja Huang, Arthur Guez, Thomas Hubert, Lucas Baker, Matthew Lai, Adrian Bolton, Yutian Chen, Timothy Lillicrap, Fan Hui, Laurent Sifre, George van den Driessche, Thore Graepel, Demis Hassabis
venue Nature citations 10,576 topic Post-training & RL source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-07-19 · published as a non-arXiv Nature article; date placement uses the Nature publication month (2017-10), not an arXiv id
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The original AlphaGo — the program that beat European champion Fan Hui in October 2015 ("AlphaGo Fan") and world-class player Lee Sedol in March 2016 ("AlphaGo Lee") — was a genuine breakthrough, but not a tabula-rasa one. Its policy network was initialized by supervised learning on human expert moves (games scraped from the KGS server), then refined with policy-gradient self-play; its separate value network was trained to predict outcomes of games played by that policy network against itself. At inference time, both networks were combined with Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) that also leaned on a hand-crafted fast rollout policy to evaluate leaf positions via Monte Carlo rollouts.

That pipeline works, but it leaves an uncomfortable dependency: human game records. Expert data sets of this kind are expensive to obtain, may be unreliable, and — more fundamentally — can impose a ceiling on performance, since a policy trained to imitate humans has no obvious reason to ever play better than the humans it imitates. It also leaves open how much of AlphaGo's strength came from the algorithm versus from the hand-engineered Go-specific features (liberties, ladders, and similar human domain knowledge) baked into its input representation and rollout policy.

The question this paper asks is blunt: strip all of that away — no human game records, no hand-crafted input features beyond the raw board, no separate rollout policy — and see whether a single network, starting from random play and improving purely through self-play reinforcement learning against the rules of the game alone, can still reach (or exceed) superhuman strength.

// how it works

The method

AlphaGo Zero replaces AlphaGo's two networks (policy + value) with one: a deep residual network fθ(s) = (p, v) that takes the raw board position and a short history of it as input — just black and white stone planes, no liberties, ladders, or other hand-engineered features — and outputs a move-probability vector p and a scalar value v estimating the current player's win probability. The network stacks many residual blocks of convolutional layers with batch normalization and ReLU nonlinearities — architecturally, an ImageNet-style vision backbone repurposed for a board position.

The key move is what MCTS is used for. Rather than treating tree search purely as a way to pick the next move at play time, the paper treats it as a policy-improvement operator baked into training: because the search's visit-count distribution π is always a stronger player than the network's raw policy p, projecting the network back onto π — and onto the self-play outcome z — is itself a training signal. Iterated over and over, this is a classic policy-iteration loop, except both the "improve" and "evaluate" steps are performed by the same MCTS acting on the same network. As the paper puts it, AlphaGo becomes its own teacher.

(p, v) = fθ(s)  →  MCTS(fθ) = π, z  →  train fθ to match (π, z) // the network is trained to imitate its own search-augmented self, then plays the next generation of self-play games

Each self-play iteration generates 25,000 games using 1,600 MCTS simulations per move (about 0.4 s of "thinking" per move); the first 30 moves of each game sample proportionally to visit count (for diversity), the rest play the strongest move deterministically. Dirichlet noise is added to the root's priors to keep exploration alive, and a resignation threshold — auto-tuned to keep false-resignation cases under 5% — cuts off clearly lost games to save compute. A new network checkpoint only becomes the "best" player used to generate further self-play data once it beats the current best by more than a 55% margin over 400 evaluation games, which keeps the whole self-improving loop from drifting on noise.

To confirm the architecture choice mattered as much as the algorithm, the authors held the training data fixed (self-play games from a 72-hour-trained AlphaGo Zero) and compared four network variants — separate vs. combined policy/value networks, and AlphaGo Lee's plain convolutional stack vs. AlphaGo Zero's residual stack:

The paper is notably explicit about what "without human knowledge" does not mean. In the Methods, the authors enumerate every piece of domain knowledge AlphaGo Zero still relies on: (1) perfect knowledge of the game's rules, used by MCTS to simulate the result of a move sequence and to score terminal positions; (2) Tromp–Taylor scoring during self-play (chosen because human scoring rules are ill-defined before territory is settled); (3) an input representation structured as a 19×19 image, so the network's architecture is matched to the board's grid; and (4) Go's invariance to rotation, reflection, and (aside from komi) colour transposition, which is exploited via data augmentation and a player-relative board representation. Beyond those four items — no rollout policy, no move exclusions (not even the standard heuristic of forbidding a player from filling their own eyes), no other Go-specific rules anywhere in the search.

// what they found

The results

The headline run used a 20-residual-block network, trained unattended for approximately three days, generating 4.9 million games of self-play. Its Elo rating passed AlphaGo Lee's after just 36 hours. At the 72-hour mark, the authors formally evaluated it against the exact AlphaGo Lee binary that beat Lee Sedol, under the same 2-hour time controls used in the Seoul man–machine match — and on a single machine with 4 TPUs, versus AlphaGo Lee's 48 TPUs spread across many machines, AlphaGo Zero won 100 games to 0.

A second, larger run — 40 residual blocks, run continuously for approximately 40 days, generating 29 million self-play games from 3.1 million mini-batches — was then evaluated in an internal round-robin against every prior AlphaGo version plus older open-source Go programs, all given 5 seconds of thinking time per move.

ProgramElovs. AlphaGo Zero
Raw network only (no search)3,055−2,130
AlphaGo Fan3,144−2,041
AlphaGo Lee3,739−1,446
AlphaGo Master4,858−327
AlphaGo Zero (40 blocks, 40 days)5,185—

AlphaGo Master — the strongest prior program, built on this same algorithm and residual architecture but bootstrapped with human data and hand-crafted features, and which had already beaten the world's top human professionals 60 games to 0 online in January 2017 — was also played head-to-head against AlphaGo Zero in a 100-game match under 2-hour time controls, with both programs running on a single machine with 4 TPUs. AlphaGo Zero won 89 games to 11.

Along the way, AlphaGo Zero rediscovered well-known human joseki (corner sequences) at various points in training, then moved past them to novel joseki variants that had no precedent in human play, along with fuseki (opening), tesuji (tactics), life-and-death, ko, yose (endgame), sente, and territorial judgment — all learned from first principles. One idiosyncrasy the authors flag: shicho (ladder-capture sequences), one of the very first things human beginners learn, was only understood by AlphaGo Zero much later in training than nearly everything else.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The result is real, and the authors are unusually careful about drawing the line correctly — but it's worth reading exactly where they draw it. "Without human knowledge" is a claim about training data: the network genuinely never sees a single human game. It is not a claim about zero engineering, and the paper does not pretend otherwise: the residual architecture, the MCTS-as-policy-improvement algorithm, the reward signal, the evaluator's 55%-margin gating rule, and the MCTS hyperparameters (chosen by Gaussian-process optimization against self-play performance, then re-optimized for the larger run) are all human-designed choices, tuned by the team, before a single self-play game was generated.

baselines

Every comparison point — Fan, Lee, Master — is the authors' own prior system, not an external competitor; that's simply what "the state of the art" meant for Go at the time, and the headline matches (vs. Lee, vs. Master) use matched hardware and match-realistic time controls. The wider reference programs (Crazy Stone, Pachi, GnuGo) are included only at a coarse 5-second-per-move setting, so their placement on the Elo scale says less about their ceiling than the Fan/Lee/Master comparisons do.

contamination

Not applicable in the held-out-benchmark sense — there is no fixed test set to leak into. The closer analogue is that the pipeline's own stopping and promotion criteria (the 55% evaluator margin, the resignation threshold auto-tuned to a <5% false-positive rate) are themselves hyperparameters optimized against the self-play process they gate, which is a different kind of self-referential tuning worth naming plainly.

what's missed

Elo here is a closed, internal ladder computed only from games between this specific population of programs — a rating of 5,185 doesn't map onto any external, human-calibrated scale. And the domain itself is about as favorable to self-play + MCTS as it gets: perfect information, deterministic, two-player, zero-sum, with a free and perfect simulator plus a clean terminal-scoring rule. The paper's own Methods section enumerates exactly what's still supplied — game rules, symmetry, a grid-matched input — and none of that transfers for free to domains without a known, perfect forward model.

would raise confidence

An external human-rating anchor rather than an internal ladder against the authors' own prior systems; a public account of how many machines actually generated the 4.9M/29M self-play games in parallel (the paper details the 64-GPU/19-CPU training cluster, but not the self-play game-generation footprint); and — the test this paper doesn't run itself — replication of the recipe on a second perfect-information game, which is exactly what AlphaZero (Silver et al., 2018) supplied a few months later for chess and shogi.

// bottom line

This is a legitimate, carefully-documented result: a single network, with no human game data and only the rules of Go as ground truth, out-plays every prior AlphaGo version, including the one that beat Lee Sedol, by a wide margin. But it's earned inside one of the friendliest possible settings for self-play — a perfect, free simulator and a fully observable, deterministic, two-player zero-sum game — and the "no domain knowledge" framing survives only because the authors are honest about the (small, but real) list of things still handed to it. The paper this graph already contains as its successor, MuZero, exists specifically because that perfect-simulator assumption doesn't hold almost anywhere else.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. A single network combining policy and value, trained purely by self-play reinforcement learning from random play, beat the Lee Sedol-defeating AlphaGo Lee 100 games to 0 after 3 days and 4.9 million self-play games — with zero human game records.
  2. MCTS wasn't just used to pick moves — its own search output became the training target. Because the searched policy π is always stronger than the network's raw prior p, training the network toward (π, z) is itself the improvement step, repeated every iteration.
  3. Matching human moves and playing well are different things: the supervised-learning baseline predicted human professional moves more accurately throughout training, yet lost to the self-play network within 24 hours.
  4. Scaling the same recipe (40 residual blocks, ~40 days, 29M games) reached 5,185 Elo, beating AlphaGo Master 89 games to 11 — but the authors' own Methods section is explicit that this still relies on perfect game rules, a free simulator, and Go's symmetry structure, which is precisely the assumption MuZero was later built to remove.
